content.text: The Handoff Problem in Claude Integrations

The team that builds a Claude integration proof of concept typically has deep knowledge of the system that is never written down: why specific prompt choices were made, what edge cases the system handles badly, which knowledge base documents are unreliable, what the eval framework scores actually mean, and what to do when the system behaves unexpectedly. When the PoC team hands the system to an operations team for production management — a transition that happens in most enterprise programmes — this knowledge disappears unless it is deliberately captured and transferred.

The handoff problem is structurally different from conventional software handoffs. In a conventional handoff, the system's behaviour is determined by its code, which is readable. In a Claude integration, the system's behaviour is determined by the system prompt, the knowledge base, the retrieval configuration, and the model version — artefacts that require context to interpret correctly. An operations team that inherits a Claude integration without a thorough handoff package is operating a system they do not fully understand.

The cost of an inadequate handoff is not visible immediately. It accumulates over weeks and months as the operations team makes small decisions — updating the system prompt, adding documents to the knowledge base, applying a model update — without the context to understand the downstream effects. The resulting quality degradation is often attributed to 'the AI getting worse' rather than to an inadequate handoff.
Handoff Package Contents

attrs.alt: Diagram showing PoC team deliverables on the left and operations team responsibilities on the right, connected by a handoff package arrow

content.text: A complete handoff package has nine components. Each component represents a distinct knowledge domain that the operations team must understand to operate the system effectively.
Component 1: Architecture Decision Record

The architecture decision record (ADR) documents the key design choices made during the PoC and the reasoning behind them. The operations team cannot evaluate proposed changes — or understand why the current design behaves as it does — without understanding why each major decision was made.
An ADR for a Claude integration should cover:
Model selection:

Which Claude model was chosen and why. If the choice involved a trade-off (Sonnet vs. Haiku for cost; Sonnet vs. Opus for accuracy), document the evaluation that led to the decision and the threshold at which the model selection should be reconsidered.
System prompt architecture:
Is the system prompt self-contained, or does it use tool calls, RAG, or dynamic context injection? Why was the architecture chosen? What alternatives were considered and rejected?
Knowledge base design:
For RAG integrations: how are documents chunked, what embedding model is used, what retrieval strategy (top-K, MMR, hybrid) was chosen and why? What chunk size and overlap were selected and why?
Latency vs. accuracy trade-offs:

If the current design accepts lower accuracy to meet latency requirements, document this explicitly. The operations team must know which trade-off is in place and what the correct parameters are for the alternative configuration.
Known rejected alternatives:
What approaches were tried and did not work? This prevents the operations team from re-trying approaches that the PoC team already found to be ineffective.
Component 2: System Prompt Documentation

The system prompt is the most important operational artefact of a Claude integration. A system prompt that is handed off as a single text file with no context is a ticking clock — the operations team will make changes based on symptoms without understanding root causes.
System Prompt Handoff Package
Current version with version number and changelog:
The system prompt must be version-controlled, and the handoff package must include the full change history and the rationale for each change.
Annotated prompt:

An annotated version of the prompt explaining what each section does and why each instruction is worded as it is. Annotations should specifically call out: instructions that were added to prevent known failure modes, instructions that are counterintuitive but necessary, and instructions that should never be removed without re-testing.
System prompt change protocol:

The procedure for making changes to the system prompt, including: how to propose a change, how to test it on the eval framework before deploying, what constitutes a breaking change requiring steering committee approval, and how to roll back if a change degrades quality.
Off-limits instructions:

A named list of instructions in the system prompt that must not be modified without a formal review process and sign-off from a named individual. These are typically the safety instructions, the scope constraints, and any compliance-driven rules.

SYSTEM PROMPT CHANGE PROTOCOL v1.2

Author: [name] | Approved: [BU lead, compliance owner] | Effective: [date]

CHANGE TYPES
  Minor change: wording clarification, example update, formatting
    - Can be approved by: operations lead
    - Requires: run eval suite before deploying; document in changelog
    - Deploy: after eval score confirms no regression

  Significant change: new behaviour, updated scope, revised persona
    - Can be approved by: BU lead + operations lead
    - Requires: eval suite; review against success criteria spec; 48h notice to users
    - Deploy: staged deployment (10% traffic) then full if no regression

  Breaking change: removes a capability, changes a compliance rule, updates safety instructions
    - Can be approved by: programme sponsor + compliance owner + InfoSec lead
    - Requires: full eval suite; compliance review; user communication plan
    - Deploy: coordination with steering committee; rollback plan required

NEVER MODIFY WITHOUT FORMAL REVIEW
  - Section 4 (data handling rules): compliance-driven; changes require legal sign-off
  - Section 7 (escalation triggers): changes require BU lead sign-off; test escalation path
  - Section 9 (scope boundaries): changes to scope require updated success criteria spec

ROLLBACK PROCEDURE
  - Keep N-1 and N-2 system prompt versions in repository
  - Rollback = deploy N-1 version; run eval suite; confirm quality restored
  - Rollback trigger: eval score drops > 5% after a change
Component 3: Eval Framework Documentation

The eval framework that the PoC team built during discovery and used to validate the system must be transferred with full documentation. An operations team that inherits an eval framework without understanding how it was built or how its scores should be interpreted will either run it without understanding the results or stop running it entirely.
Test set with ground truth and rationale:

Every test case must include the query, the ground truth answer, and a brief note on why this case was included (what failure mode it tests for). The rationale for edge cases is especially important — an operations team member who does not understand why an adversarial test case is in the set may remove it during a 'cleanup' pass.
Scoring rubric with worked examples:

The rubric must include worked examples showing a full-score response, a partial-score response, and a zero-score response for each scoring criterion. Without examples, different evaluators will score the same response differently.
Calibration records:

If the eval framework uses LLM-as-judge, the calibration records (human scores vs. judge scores for the calibration set, agreement statistics, and any cases where the judge was corrected) must be included. The calibration is specific to the rubric version — any rubric change requires re-calibration.
Eval schedule and ownership:

Who runs the eval? How often? What triggers an ad hoc run (a system prompt change, a knowledge base update, a model version change)? What is the alert threshold, and who is notified when it is breached?
Component 4: Known Limitations and Failure Modes

Every Claude integration has a set of query types or scenarios where it performs below the general quality bar. These are the known failure modes — edge cases, domain gaps, or structural limitations that the PoC team discovered during testing and that users may encounter in production. The operations team must know about these before they receive user reports, not after.
Failure Mode Category
Description
Handoff Documentation Required
Out-of-scope query handling
Query types the system was not designed to handle and should redirect or decline
Named examples of out-of-scope queries; expected system behaviour; how to confirm the system is redirecting correctly
Domain gaps
Topics within the nominal scope where the knowledge base is incomplete or the model has insufficient knowledge
Named gaps; known date cutoffs for knowledge; user-facing messaging when domain gaps are hit
Edge case behaviours
Specific query patterns that produce incorrect or unexpected outputs despite being within nominal scope
Query examples; current system behaviour; root cause (if known); whether a fix is planned
Context window boundary effects
Behaviour when input is near the context limit; retrieval degradation with very long documents
Threshold input sizes that trigger boundary effects; expected behaviour; how to check if a query hit a boundary
Prompt injection sensitivity
Query patterns that partially defeat prompt instructions (not full prompt injection, but softening)
Known patterns; current mitigations in the system prompt; monitoring approach

The known limitations document is the most frequently omitted component of a handoff package. PoC teams are reluctant to document limitations because it feels like admitting failure. But an operations team that learns about a limitation from an angry user report is in a worse position than one that was briefed on it during handoff and had prepared a user-facing response.
Component 5: Knowledge Base Inventory

For integrations using RAG, the knowledge base is an operational artefact that requires ongoing maintenance. The operations team must know what is in the knowledge base, where each document came from, when it was last updated, and who is responsible for keeping it current.

{
  "knowledge_base_inventory": {
    "integration": "contract-review-assistant",
    "version": "2.1",
    "total_documents": 847,
    "total_chunks": 4231,
    "embedding_model": "text-embedding-3-large",
    "chunk_size": 512,
    "chunk_overlap": 64,
    "sources": [
      {
        "source_id": "internal-policy",
        "description": "Internal contract policy and approval requirements",
        "document_count": 124,
        "last_updated": "2024-10-15",
        "update_frequency": "quarterly",
        "owner": "Legal Operations Team",
        "owner_contact": "legal-ops@company.com",
        "staleness_risk": "high — changes with regulatory updates",
        "review_trigger": "any regulatory change affecting contract terms"
      },
      {
        "source_id": "standard-clauses",
        "description": "Library of standard contract clauses with approved and non-approved variants",
        "document_count": 312,
        "last_updated": "2024-09-01",
        "update_frequency": "semi-annually",
        "owner": "General Counsel office",
        "owner_contact": "gc-team@company.com",
        "staleness_risk": "medium — updated when clause library is revised",
        "review_trigger": "clause library revision"
      }
    ],
    "indexing_pipeline": {
      "tool": "custom Python pipeline",
      "location": "repo: contracts-assistant/scripts/index_knowledge_base.py",
      "run_time_minutes": 45,
      "schedule": "weekly on Sundays 02:00 UTC",
      "last_run": "2024-11-10",
      "runbook_section": "Section 4.2"
    }
  }
}
Handoff Acceptance Criteria

The operations team does not accept the handoff by receiving the package — they accept it by demonstrating that they can operate the system independently. The handoff acceptance process includes a structured shadowing period and a set of acceptance tests:
Shadowing period (1–2 weeks):

A named operations team member shadows the PoC team during routine operations: running the eval suite, responding to a monitoring alert, making a minor system prompt change, and updating the knowledge base. They perform each task with PoC team oversight before doing it independently.
Simulated incident response:

The operations team responds to a simulated incident (a staged quality regression or system unavailability) using the runbook, with the PoC team observing but not intervening. If the operations team cannot resolve the simulated incident using the runbook, the runbook is updated before handoff is accepted.
Knowledge base update exercise:

The operations team adds a new document to the knowledge base, re-indexes, and validates that the change improved or did not degrade retrieval quality on the relevant eval cases. If they cannot complete this exercise independently, the knowledge base documentation needs supplementation.
Q&A session with PoC team:

A structured session where the operations team asks every question they have about the system. Every question that cannot be answered by the existing documentation becomes a documentation gap that the PoC team must fill before the handoff is complete.
Post-Handoff Support Period

Handoff completion does not mean the PoC team disappears. A defined post-handoff support period — typically 4–8 weeks — provides the operations team with access to PoC team expertise as they encounter their first production incidents, model updates, and knowledge base maintenance cycles.
Post-Handoff Week
PoC Team Availability
Expected Ops Team Activities
Transition Milestone
Weeks 1–2
On-call: responds to queries within 4 hours during business hours
First independent eval run; first knowledge base update; first minor system prompt change
Operations team completes first round of routine tasks without incident
Weeks 3–4
Available: responds to queries within 1 business day
First model update evaluation; first user feedback triage cycle
Operations team handles first user-reported quality issue independently
Weeks 5–8
Advisory: responds to queries within 3 business days
First quarterly review pack prepared independently
Operations team prepares and presents first quarterly review without PoC team involvement
After week 8
Escalation only: available for novel architectural questions
Full independent operation
Handoff formally complete; PoC team formally disengaged from operations

The post-handoff support period is a budget line item, not an informal arrangement. PoC team members' time during this period should be explicitly allocated in the programme plan. Without formal allocation, the support period is crowded out by other commitments and the operations team is effectively abandoned at a critical point in their learning curve.
The Runbook as a Living Document

The runbook is the single most important operational document for a Claude integration. It must be complete enough that a new operations team member can execute every routine task and respond to every alert without asking the PoC team for help. Writing a runbook that achieves this standard requires deliberate effort — the natural tendency is to write procedures that make sense to the person who already knows the system, not the person who will be reading it under pressure at 11pm during an incident.
Runbook Structure for Claude Integrations
System overview:

One page describing what the integration does, who uses it, what it connects to, and what happens to users if it fails. This section is read by the on-call engineer who is not familiar with the integration and needs to understand the stakes before starting diagnosis.
Architecture diagram:

A current diagram showing all components: the Claude API, the application server, the knowledge base (if RAG), any queues or caches, the user interface, and the authentication layer. Diagrams that show what used to exist, or what was planned but not built, are worse than no diagram.
Routine operations:

Step-by-step procedures for every routine task: running the eval suite, updating the knowledge base, making a system prompt change, reviewing cost alerts, and producing the monthly metrics report. Each procedure has a named expected outcome so the operator knows when they have completed it correctly.
Alert response procedures:

For every monitoring alert, a runbook section that describes: what the alert means, what to check first, the most common causes and their fixes, when to escalate, and what to do if the runbook procedure does not resolve the alert. Alert procedures must be indexed by the alert name exactly as it appears in the monitoring tool.
Emergency procedures:

Procedures for the highest-severity scenarios: complete system unavailability, data breach suspected, model producing harmful outputs, API credentials compromised. These procedures must be verified to work by a dry run before handoff — not just written and assumed.
Credential and access management:

Where every credential is stored, who has access, how to rotate credentials, and what happens to access when a team member leaves. Credentials stored in the runbook itself (even in a secret-scanning-protected repository) are a security risk — the runbook should reference a secrets management system, not contain the secrets.
Runbook Quality Check
Before accepting the runbook as part of the handoff, the operations team should run the following quality check:
Have an operations team member who was NOT involved in writing the runbook follow each procedure from start to finish. If they ask a clarifying question or get stuck, the runbook has a gap.
For each alert procedure: does it reference a specific monitoring dashboard or log query that shows the relevant data? Vague procedures ('check the logs') are not actionable under pressure.

For the system prompt change procedure: does it include the exact commands to deploy the change and to verify the deployment? Procedures that end at 'upload the new version' without a verification step are incomplete.
For the rollback procedure: has it actually been tested by deploying a prior version and confirming the system returns to its previous state? An untested rollback is not a rollback.
Pilot Metrics and Lessons Learned

The pilot metrics and lessons learned report is the PoC team's most valuable contribution to the operations team's understanding of the system. It describes not just what happened during the pilot, but what was learned — what assumptions were wrong, what surprised the team, what user behaviour was not anticipated, and what the operations team should watch for in production.

PILOT METRICS AND LESSONS LEARNED REPORT
Integration: Contract Review Assistant | Pilot period: 2024-09-01 to 2024-10-31
PoC lead: [name] | Operations lead: [name]

PILOT METRICS SUMMARY
  North-star (contract cycle time): baseline 12.4 days → pilot end 8.9 days (28% reduction)
  Target: 7.5 days by 90 days post go-live. On track.
  Issue identification recall (critical issues): 91% (target: 88%) — above target
  Issue identification recall (material issues): 82% (target: 85%) — below target, improving
  Revision cycle rate: 18% (target: 15%) — slightly above target; root cause identified

WHAT WORKED BETTER THAN EXPECTED
  - Users adopted Claude's suggested issue labels faster than anticipated (day 3, not day 10)
  - Out-of-scope query redirect rate was 97% (better than the 80% we expected)
  - Material issue detection improved from 78% to 82% over the pilot period as knowledge base
    was refined — expect continued improvement in production

WHAT DID NOT WORK AS EXPECTED
  - Revision cycle rate is 18%, above the 15% target. Root cause: users are accepting Claude's
    issue identification but writing their own remediation notes, which reviewers then revise.
    Mitigation underway: adding remediation note templates to the knowledge base.
  - Three power users asked the system questions well outside its scope (M&A due diligence
    queries). The system correctly redirected, but the users were frustrated. Recommend:
    add a brief scope reminder to the onboarding message.

KNOWLEDGE BASE LEARNINGS
  - 14 documents in the initial KB were found to be outdated; replaced during pilot
  - The "standard-clauses" section had poor retrieval precision for multi-jurisdictional queries
    — fixed by splitting into jurisdiction-specific sub-sections (retrieval precision improved
    from 0.71 to 0.84 on the relevant eval cases)
  - KB update process took 45 minutes on first run; subsequent runs are 12 minutes as the
    pipeline only re-indexes changed documents

SYSTEM PROMPT CHANGES MADE DURING PILOT
  - v1.0 to v1.1: Added jurisdiction detection instruction (improved cross-border query handling)
  - v1.1 to v1.2: Tightened scope boundary instruction after M&A query incidents
  - v1.2 to v1.3: Added remediation note template reference (currently being evaluated)

OPEN ISSUES HANDED OVER TO OPERATIONS
  - OI-001: Material issue recall at 82%, below 85% target. Owner: ops team.
    Action: weekly eval run; escalate to PoC team if not at 85% by 2024-12-15.
  - OI-002: KB staleness alert not yet implemented. Owner: ops team.
    Action: implement alert by 2024-11-30 per runbook section 6.3.
Handoff in Programmes Without a Dedicated Operations Team

Many enterprise Claude integrations, especially in their early stages, do not have a dedicated operations team. The BU lead or a product owner takes on operational responsibilities alongside their day job. This context changes the handoff requirements — not by reducing them, but by adapting them to the available capacity.
When the operations function is not a dedicated team:
Simplify the eval cadence:

A weekly automated eval run with an email report is achievable for a part-time operations function; a manual eval with human scoring every week is not. Invest the upfront effort to automate the eval pipeline so that the ongoing operational effort is monitoring the automated results rather than running the eval manually.
Shorten the runbook:

A 40-page runbook that a full-time operations engineer might manage is inaccessible to a BU lead with other responsibilities. Identify the 5 most important procedures and the 5 most common alerts and write those sections in detail; put everything else in an appendix that can be consulted if needed.
Extend the post-handoff support period:

A part-time operations function will take longer to build competence than a dedicated team. The post-handoff support period should be extended to 12 weeks with explicit availability from the PoC team during weeks 1–8.
Define a clear escalation path to the PoC team:

For AI-specific issues that the BU lead cannot resolve independently (architectural problems, significant quality regression, security incidents), there must be a clear escalation path to someone with PoC-level expertise. This person may be from a central AI platform team, an external consultant, or a member of the PoC team who has agreed to remain available in an advisory capacity.

The worst operational outcome is a part-time operations function that is not confident in its own ability to make decisions, so it avoids all maintenance activities (system prompt updates, knowledge base refreshes, eval runs) until the system drifts far from its design intent. Better to design a minimal operational model that the BU lead can sustain than to hand off a full operational model that they cannot.
Handoff Documentation Anti-Patterns
Common failure modes in handoff documentation that produce inadequate transfers:
Anti-Pattern
How It Manifests
Consequence
Prevention
The README handoff
PoC team hands over the code repository with a README and considers the handoff complete
Operations team has no context for design decisions, no runbook, no failure mode documentation
Require all nine handoff package components as conditions of PoC completion; include in project plan
Tribal knowledge appendix
Runbook references 'check with [PoC team member name]' for complex scenarios
Handoff is not actually complete; the system remains dependent on PoC team member availability
Every 'ask [name]' reference must be replaced with a written procedure before handoff is accepted
Demo-quality documentation
ADR and runbook written for the demo presentation, not for operational use; incomplete, visually polished, not actionable
Operations team follows the polished document to a dead end and has to improvise
Operations team member reads every document as part of acceptance; every dead end is flagged before handoff complete
Knowledge base without ownership
Knowledge base handed over without named owners for each content area
Content goes stale; operations team does not know who to ask when a document needs updating
KB inventory with named owners and update schedules required before handoff accepted
No known limitations document
PoC team considers documenting limitations to be admitting failure
Operations team learns about failure modes from user complaints; cannot prepare appropriate responses
Known limitations document is a required handoff component; zero-entry document is a red flag, not a pass
Summary

The operational handoff from a PoC team to an operations team requires a nine-component package: architecture decision record, system prompt documentation with change protocol, eval framework with rubric and calibration records, known limitations and failure modes, knowledge base inventory, runbook, pilot metrics and lessons learned, open issues register, and escalation contacts. Handoff is accepted through a structured process including a shadowing period, simulated incident response, knowledge base update exercise, and Q&A session — not by receiving documents. A post-handoff support period of 4–8 weeks with defined availability levels ensures the operations team has access to PoC expertise as they encounter their first production challenges. The cost of an inadequate handoff accumulates silently in quality degradation and misattributed operational decisions.
